Webhooks🚨 The Problem: “Are we there yet?” (a.k.a. constant API polling)
Imagine you’re ordering pizza.
But instead of the restaurant calling you when it’s ready, you call them every 10 seconds:
“Is it ready yet?”
“Now?”
“What about now?”
“I’m literally starving, any updates?”
That’s basically polling in system design.
In software terms:

• Your system keeps asking another system:
“Any new data? Any update? Any update? Any update?”

• Even when nothing changed.
💀 Result:

• Wasted CPU

• Wasted network

• Sad servers

• Angry engineers
This becomes a nightmare in:

• payment systems

• Git events

• delivery tracking

• notifications

• any event-driven system
So we needed a better idea.
🧠 The Solution: Webhooks (a.k.a. “I’ll notify YOU when I’m ready”)
Instead of constantly asking:
“Is it ready yet?”
We flip the logic:
“Hey, when something happens, just call me.”
That’s a webhook.
🎯 Simple definition:
A webhook is an HTTP request sent automatically from one system to another when an event happens.
No asking.
No polling.
No anxiety.
Just vibes.
🍕 Real-life analogy: Pizza with dignity
❌ Polling:
You call pizza shop every 10 seconds:

• “Ready?”

• “Ready?”

• “Ready?”
✅ Webhook:
Pizza shop says:
“Leave your number. I’ll call you when it’s ready.”
And boom:
📞 “Your pizza is ready.”
That’s it. You live your life. You stop being annoying.
⚙️ How Webhooks Work (System Design Breakdown)
Let’s go technical — but still fun.
🧩 Actors:

• Producer (Service A) → the system where something happens
Example: payment system like Stripe

• Consumer (Service B) → your application
Example: your backend server
🔁 Step-by-step flow:
1. You register a webhook URL
You tell Stripe (or GitHub, etc.):
“When payment succeeds, call this URL:
https://myapp.com/webhook/payments”
2. Something happens
A user pays 💳
Stripe detects:
“Payment succeeded!”
3. Stripe sends an HTTP request
Stripe sends a POST request to your server:
POST /webhook/payments HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: application/json{
"event": "payment_success",
"user_id": 123,
"amount": 50
}
4. Your server reacts
Your backend:

• validates request

• updates DB

• sends email

• triggers shipping

• celebrates quietly 🎉
🧪 Example 1: GitHub Webhooks (Dev life saver)
When someone pushes code:
GitHub Webhooks Docs
GitHub sends:
{
"action": "push",
"repository": "awesome-project",
"pusher": "dev123"
}Your CI/CD system reacts:

• runs tests

• deploys app

• maybe breaks production (optional feature)
🧪 Example 2: E-commerce order system
Customer places order 🛒
Webhook triggers:

• payment confirmed

• inventory updated

• shipping label created

• SMS sent
No polling. No stress. Just event-driven happiness.
🧪 Example 3: Delivery tracking apps
Instead of asking:
“Where is my package?”
DHL sends updates like:

• “Picked up”

• “In transit”

• “Out for delivery”

• “Delivered”
Your app updates UI automatically.
🧠 Inside the Engine: What really matters in system design
Webhooks are simple, but production-grade systems add complexity:
1. Retry mechanism 🔁
If your server is down:

• webhook retries

• exponential backoff

• eventual delivery
2. Security 🔐
You must verify:

• signature (HMAC)

• secret tokens

• source validation
Otherwise… anyone could fake “payment success” 😬
3. Idempotency 🧱
Same webhook might arrive twice.
So your system must ensure:
“I processed this event already”
Otherwise:

• double emails

• double charges

• chaos
4. Queue systems 🧵
Many systems don’t process directly.
They push webhook events into:

• Kafka

• RabbitMQ

• SQS (AWS)
Then workers handle it safely.
⚖️ Webhooks vs Polling (final showdown)
FeaturePolling 😩Webhooks 🚀EfficiencyBadGreatLatencySlowFastServer loadHighLowComplexitySimpleMediumScalabilityWeakStrong
Polling is like:
“Hey, any update? any update? any update?”
Webhooks are like:
“I got you. I’ll tell you when something happens.”
⚔️ Bonus: Webhooks vs WebSockets (final boss comparison)
Now let’s bring in the cousin everyone confuses them with: WebSockets
🔌 WebSockets = Persistent live connection
Instead of one-time HTTP calls:

• client and server stay connected

• both can send messages anytime
Used for:

• chat apps 💬

• multiplayer games 🎮

• live dashboards 📊
Webhooks vs 🔌 WebSockets
FeatureWebhooksWebSocketsConnectionOne-time HTTP callPersistent connectionDirectionServer → ServerBi-directionalUse caseEvents (payments, Git, APIs)Real-time appsScalabilityEasierMore complexStateStatelessStateful
🧠 Simple memory trick:

• Webhooks = “I’ll call you when I need you”

• WebSockets = “We stay on call forever”
🎯 Final thought
Webhooks are basically the universe telling developers:
“Stop asking every 5 seconds. I’ll text you.”
They power:

• payments (Stripe) Stripe

• developer automation (GitHub) GitHub

• modern event-driven architectures (AWS) AWS Event-Driven Architecture
And they make systems:

• faster

• cheaper

• cleaner

• less annoying

If systems were relationships:

• Polling = clingy ex 😬

• Webhooks = respectful partner who texts when needed ❤️

• WebSockets = “we live together now” situation 🏠
